The table is detailed, but the interaction is easier to understand with predictions.

plot_predictions(price_interaction, condition = c("accommodates", "room_type")) +
  labs(
    title = "Interaction Model: Capacity and Room Type",
    x = "Guests accommodated",
    y = "Predicted log price",
    color = "Room type"
  ) +
  theme_bw()








When presenting an interaction, a prediction plot is usually clearer than a table of interaction coefficients.
The interaction plot tells us that the capacity-price relationship is steeper for entire homes than for private rooms. Each additional guest adds roughly 10% to the price of an entire home but only about 6% to a private room (the main capacity coefficient is about 0.10, and the interaction term is about −0.04, so the private-room slope is roughly 0.06). The interaction coefficient is significant at conventional levels. The substantive direction is sensible: the marginal value of accommodating one more person is larger when the whole property is yours.

title: 11  Visualizing Models
text:
11.9 Logit: binary outcomes
A logistic regression model is used when the outcome is binary. We will define a binary outcome: whether a listing is expensive (price of $150 or more per night, the top quartile in this data).
The function glm() is part of base R. With family = binomial, it fits a logistic regression model.

price_logit <- glm(
  expensive ~ accommodates + room_type + log_reviews + minimum_stay,
  data = airbnb_interaction,
  family = binomial
)

The model estimates log-odds. Log-odds are not intuitive for most readers.

modelsummary(
  price_logit,
  stars = TRUE,
  coef_omit = "Intercept",
  gof_omit = "AIC|BIC|Log.Lik|RMSE"
)



    

    

    
    
    
      
        
        
              
                 
                (1)
              
        
        + p < 0.1, * p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, *** p < 0.001
        
                
                  accommodates
                  0.390***
                
                
                  
                  (0.042)
                
                
                  room_typePrivate room
                  -2.842***
                
                
                  
                  (0.378)
                
                
                  log_reviews
                  -0.247**
                
                
                  
                  (0.081)
                
                
                  minimum_stay
                  0.073
                
                
                  
                  (0.060)
                
                
                  Num.Obs.
                  1376
                
                
                  F
                  46.531
                
        
      
    



A coefficient plot is still possible, but the x-axis is in log-odds.

modelplot(price_logit) +
  labs(
    title = "Coefficient Plot for a Logit Model",
    x = "Estimated association in log-odds",
    y = NULL
  ) +
  theme_bw()








For interpretation, predicted probabilities are better.

plot_predictions(price_logit, condition = c("accommodates", "room_type")) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format()) +
  labs(
    title = "Predicted Probability of an Expensive Listing",
    subtitle = "Expensive defined as price ≥ $150",
    x = "Guests accommodated",
    y = "Predicted probability",
    color = "Room type"
  ) +
  theme_bw()








This plot returns the model to a scale people understand: probability. The story is stark. An entire home that accommodates eight guests has roughly a 90% predicted probability of being expensive. A private room at the same capacity has well under 10%. The two curves separate quickly even at low capacity. The natural reading is that room type matters far more than capacity for whether a listing ends up in the top quartile.

title: 11  Visualizing Models
text:
11.10 Marginal and adjusted predictions
There are two related ideas that often appear in model visualization:

Conditional predictions hold variables at selected values and show predictions across a grid.
Marginal or adjusted predictions compute predictions for observed cases and average across a group.

The by argument gives averaged predictions by group.

plot_predictions(price_logit, by = "room_type") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format()) +
  labs(
    title = "Average Predicted Probability of an Expensive Listing by Room Type",
    x = "Room type",
    y = "Average predicted probability"
  ) +
  theme_bw()








Use conditional predictions when you want to show a relationship across values. Use averaged predictions when you want a compact group comparison.
The averaged predictions make the gap concrete: about 36% of entire homes are predicted to be expensive, versus roughly 1% of private rooms. This is averaged across the observed distribution of capacity in each group, so it reflects the kinds of listings that actually exist rather than predictions at arbitrary capacity values.

title: 11  Visualizing Models
text:
11.11 Slopes and marginal effects
Prediction plots show predicted outcomes. Sometimes the quantity of interest is the slope: how much the predicted outcome changes when a predictor changes.
For the interaction model, the capacity slope is allowed to differ by room type. plot_slopes() visualizes those room-type-specific slopes directly.
plot_slopes() comes from the marginaleffects package.

plot_slopes(
  price_interaction,
  variables = "accommodates",
  condition = "room_type"
) +
  labs(
    title = "Estimated Capacity Slope by Room Type",
    x = "Room type",
    y = "Estimated slope for guest capacity"
  ) +
  theme_bw()








The slopes plot makes the interaction story numerical: the estimated capacity slope is about 0.10 for entire homes and about 0.06 for private rooms. The two intervals are close but the entire-home slope is clearly higher. The same finding could be read out of the interaction-model table, but the plot makes the size, direction, and uncertainty visible in one frame.
This is a model-based comparison. It should be read as an estimate from the fitted model, not as a raw descriptive difference.

title: 11  Visualizing Models
text:
11.12 Comparing plots
The patchwork package can place related model plots side by side.

p_coef <- modelplot(price_model) +
  labs(title = "Coefficients", x = "Estimate", y = NULL) +
  theme_bw()

p_pred <- plot_predictions(price_model, condition = c("accommodates", "room_type")) +
  labs(title = "Predictions", x = "Guests accommodated", y = "Log price") +
  theme_bw()

p_coef + p_pred








The coefficient plot emphasizes estimates and uncertainty. The prediction plot emphasizes substantive interpretation.

title: 12  Mapping
text:
12.1 A blank US map
Maps are useful when geography is part of the question. They are not automatically better than tables or charts. Use a map when location, region, distance, or spatial clustering matters.
This chapter covers usmap for United States maps and tmap for world maps. The extras introduce tidycensus for Census data and statebins for equal-area tile maps. Lower-level spatial tools (sf, rnaturalearth, leaflet) are in the companion experiment file.
plot_usmap() creates a map with very little code.
plot_usmap()
The package handles the map geometry and projection for us. This is a good first tool when the goal is a United States state or county map.

title: 12  Mapping
text:
12.2 State and county maps
The default map shows states. County maps use regions = "counties".

plot_usmap(regions = "counties")








County maps contain many small shapes. They can be useful, but they can also become visually busy. Use county maps when county-level variation is central to the question.

title: 12  Mapping
12  Mapping
text:
12.4 FIPS codes
FIPS codes are standardized geographic identifiers. State FIPS codes have two digits. County FIPS codes have five digits: two for the state and three for the county.

fips(state = "Massachusetts")

[1] "25"



fips_info(c("25", "26", "27"))


  



County FIPS lookups work the same way.

fips_info(c("25025", "26163", "27053"))


  



FIPS codes matter because many government and social science datasets use them as join keys. A map will not know that “Massachusetts”, “MA”, and "25" refer to the same place unless you create a common identifier.

title: 12  Mapping
text:
12.5 A state choropleth
A choropleth map fills geographic units by a data value. usmap includes a state population dataset.

data(statepop, package = "usmap")

statepop |>
  select(full, abbr, fips, pop_2022) |>
  slice_head(n = 8)


  



Now map 2022 population.

plot_usmap(data = statepop, values = "pop_2022", color = "white") +
  scale_fill_continuous(
    low = "white",
    high = "steelblue",
    name = "Population",
    labels = scales::comma
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "US State Populations",
    subtitle = "2022 estimates"
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "right")








Population maps often emphasize large states. That may be the point, but it may also hide other patterns. Always ask whether the mapped value should be a total, a rate, a percentage, or a change.

title: 12  Mapping
text:
12.6 Focused regional maps
The include argument limits the map to selected states.

plot_usmap(
  data = statepop,
  values = "pop_2022",
  include = c("CA", "ID", "NV", "OR", "WA"),
  color = "white"
) +
  scale_fill_continuous(
    low = "white",
    high = "darkgreen",
    name = "Population",
    labels = scales::comma
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Population in Selected Western States",
    subtitle = "2022 estimates"
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "right")








usmap also has predefined region objects.

plot_usmap(include = .south_region)








You can combine inclusion and exclusion.

plot_usmap(include = .south_region, exclude = .east_south_central)

title: 12  Mapping
text:
12.7 County choropleths
usmap includes county poverty data.

data(countypov, package = "usmap")

countypov |>
  select(fips, abbr, county, pct_pov_2021) |>
  slice_head(n = 8)


  



County maps require regions = "counties".

plot_usmap(
  data = countypov,
  values = "pct_pov_2021",
  regions = "counties",
  include = c("CT", "ME", "MA", "NH", "RI", "VT"),
  color = "white",
  size = 0.1
) +
  scale_fill_continuous(
    low = "white",
    high = "firebrick",
    name = "Poverty rate",
    labels = scales::label_percent(scale = 1)
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "County Poverty Rates in New England",
    subtitle = "2021 estimates"
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "right")








County maps work best when the color scale and the legend are easy to read. Thin borders and focused regions can help.

title: 12  Mapping
text:
12.8 Joining your own data
Most mapping projects follow the same pattern:

Get a data frame with a geographic identifier such as a state abbreviation or FIPS code.
Clean the identifier so it matches the map data.
Join or pass the data to a mapping function.
Choose a color scale and labels that match the substance of the variable.

Here is a small state-level example.

state_example <- tibble(
  state = c("CA", "TX", "FL", "NY", "IL", "PA", "OH", "GA"),
  example_rate = c(12.3, 10.8, 14.1, 11.7, 13.4, 12.8, 13.1, 15.0)
)

state_example


  



plot_usmap() can use state abbreviations directly when the data have a state column.

plot_usmap(data = state_example, values = "example_rate", color = "white") +
  scale_fill_continuous(
    low = "white",
    high = "navy",
    name = "Example rate"
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Example State-Level Data",
    subtitle = "Only states in the example data are filled"
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "right")








For county data, make sure FIPS codes remain character strings. Do not let leading zeroes disappear.

county_example <- tibble(
  fips = c("25025", "26163", "17031"),
  value = c(41, 38, 44)
)

county_example

title: 12  Mapping
text:
12.9 Choosing map colors
Sequential color scales work well for ordered values such as population, income, or percentages. viridis scales are a good default because they are readable and colorblind-friendlier than many rainbow palettes.

plot_usmap(data = statepop, values = "pop_2022", color = "white") +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(
    option = "magma",
    name = "Population",
    labels = scales::comma
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "US State Populations",
    subtitle = "A viridis sequential color scale"
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "right")








Use diverging color scales when the variable has a meaningful midpoint, such as change around zero or an index centered at an average.

title: 12  Mapping
text:
12.10 Short exercise
Use statepop.

Make a state population map with a different sequential color scale.
Focus the map on one region or a custom set of states.
Change the title, subtitle, and legend title.
Write one sentence explaining whether a map is better than a sorted bar chart for this variable.

title: 12  Mapping
text:
12.11 Extra: ACS data with tidycensus
The tidycensus package can download American Community Survey data. This is optional because it may require a Census API key and internet access.

library(tidycensus)

county_income <- get_acs(
  geography = "county",
  variables = "B19013_001",
  year = 2022,
  geometry = FALSE
) |>
  transmute(
    fips = GEOID,
    income = estimate
  )

plot_usmap(
  data = county_income,
  values = "income",
  regions = "counties",
  include = .midwest_region,
  color = "white",
  size = 0.1
) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(
    name = "Median household income",
    labels = scales::dollar
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Median Household Income by County",
    subtitle = "ACS estimates"
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "right")

This workflow is useful for public policy, sociology, political science, and demography projects because Census data use standard geographic identifiers.

title: 12  Mapping
section: 12.12 Extra: equal-area state tile maps with statebins
text:
12.12 Extra: equal-area state tile maps with statebins
Traditional maps give more visual space to geographically large states. A tile map gives every state the same visual size.

library(statebins)

USArrests |>
  rownames_to_column("state") |>
  statebins(value_col = "Assault", name = "Assault", round = TRUE) +
  theme_statebins(legend_position = "right") +
  labs(title = "Assault Arrests by State")

State bins are not geographically precise, but they are useful when equal visual weight is more important than exact shape.

title: 12  Mapping
text:
12.13 Extra: world maps with tmap
The tmap package is built specifically for thematic maps. Its grammar resembles ggplot2 — you start with a shape layer and add styled layers on top — but the defaults are tuned for cartography: sensible projections, automatic legends, and built-in support for switching between static and interactive maps with a single line.
tmap ships with a World dataset (an sf object covering 177 countries with indicators like population, life expectancy, and GDP per capita). That makes it a good entry point for country-level visualization without separately loading natural-earth data.

library(tmap)
library(sf)

data(World)

tmap_mode("plot")  # static maps

tm_shape(World) +
  tm_polygons(fill = "life_exp", fill.scale = tm_scale_continuous(values = "viridis")) +
  tm_title("Life Expectancy by Country") +
  tm_layout(legend.outside = TRUE)

tm_shape() declares the geometry. tm_polygons() fills the countries by a variable. tm_scale_continuous() controls the color ramp. The structure parallels ggplot() + geom_sf() + scale_fill_*(), but the components are map-specific.
A categorical fill works the same way.

tm_shape(World) +
  tm_polygons(
    fill = "continent",
    fill.scale = tm_scale_categorical(values = "brewer.set3")
  ) +
  tm_title("Countries by Continent") +
  tm_layout(legend.outside = TRUE)

World maps benefit from a thoughtful projection. The default Mercator-style projection inflates high latitudes; Equal Earth (EPSG:8857) preserves area, which is usually what you want for global comparisons.

World_ea <- st_transform(World, crs = "+proj=eqearth")

tm_shape(World_ea) +
  tm_polygons(
    fill = "gdp_cap_est",
    fill.scale = tm_scale_continuous(values = "plasma"),
    fill.legend = tm_legend(title = "GDP per capita")
  ) +
  tm_title("GDP Per Capita on an Equal-Earth Projection") +
  tm_layout(legend.outside = TRUE)

tmap can also switch into interactive mode with one line. The same map code is reused; the renderer changes.

tmap_mode("view")  # interactive

tm_shape(World) +
  tm_polygons(
    fill = "life_exp",
    fill.scale = tm_scale_continuous(values = "viridis"),
    popup.vars = c("Country" = "name", "Life expectancy" = "life_exp", "GDP/capita" = "gdp_cap_est")
  )

tmap_mode("plot")  # switch back

That dual-mode behavior is the main reason to use tmap over plain ggplot2 + geom_sf for world maps. The same syntax produces a static figure for a paper and an interactive map for a webpage. You can also join your own country-level data to World by iso_a3 (a three-letter country code) and map any variable that way.

12.13.1 Zooming into a region
To show a specific continent, filter the World sf object before passing it to tm_shape(). This removes all other countries from the frame entirely.

# Russia spans the antimeridian (full globe width) and France includes French
# Guiana (far southwest). Excluding Russia and cropping to the European extent
# keeps the map focused on the European landmass.
europe <- World[World$continent == "Europe" & World$name != "Russia", ] |>
  sf::st_crop(xmin = -25, ymin = 34, xmax = 45, ymax = 72)

tm_shape(europe) +
  tm_polygons(
    fill = "gdp_cap_est",
    fill.scale = tm_scale_continuous(values = "plasma"),
    fill.legend = tm_legend(title = "GDP per capita")
  ) +
  tm_text("name", size = 0.45) +
  tm_crs("auto") +
  tm_title("GDP Per Capita in Europe") +
  tm_layout(legend.outside = TRUE)

tm_crs("auto") lets tmap choose a suitable projection for the region — for Europe it typically selects something like ETRS89-LAEA, which minimises distortion at European latitudes. tm_text() adds country name labels; keep size small for regions with many small countries.
The same pattern works for any continent. Replace "Europe" with "Africa", "Asia", "South America", or "North America".

africa <- World[World$continent == "Africa", ]

tm_shape(africa) +
  tm_polygons(
    fill = "well_being",
    fill.scale = tm_scale_continuous(values = "brewer.yl_or_rd"),
    fill.legend = tm_legend(title = "Well-being index")
  ) +
  tm_crs("auto") +
  tm_title("Well-Being Index in Africa") +
  tm_layout(legend.outside = TRUE)

title: 13  Agentic AI and Next Steps
text:
13.1 What AI can help with
Large language models can help with coding, data analysis, visualization, writing, and debugging. They can also produce confident mistakes. The goal is not to replace your judgment. The goal is to use AI as an assistant while keeping you responsible for the analysis.
This chapter focuses on practical workflows: how to ask for help, how to review answers, how to protect privacy, and how to use agentic tools without losing control of your project.
AI tools are most useful when the task has enough structure for you to check the answer.
Common visualization uses include:
AI is less reliable when the task requires facts that may have changed, private data it cannot see, or judgment without enough context.

title: 13  Agentic AI and Next Steps
text:
explaining unfamiliar R code
finding the source of an error message
suggesting a cleaner ggplot2 pipeline
proposing labels, captions, or alternative chart types
converting a rough plot into a more polished plot
reviewing a table or figure for readability
writing short helper functions
drafting Quarto prose around code output
checking whether a project depends on files that are not included
crumbs: 13  Agentic AI and Next Steps

title: 13  Agentic AI and Next Steps
text:
13.2 Web chatbots
Web chatbots are the most familiar AI interface. You paste a question, code, data description, or error message into a browser and get a response.
This works well for short, self-contained questions.
Example prompt:
I am using R and ggplot2. This code produces a plot, but the legend title is unclear.
Please explain what each line does and suggest a more readable version.

<paste code here>
For debugging, include the error message and the smallest code example that reproduces the problem.
I get this error in R:

<paste exact error>

Here is the code that triggered it:

<paste code>

Please identify the likely cause. Do not rewrite the whole script unless needed.
The main limitation of web chat is context. If the model cannot see your full project, it may guess incorrectly about data names, packages, or earlier steps.
crumbs: 13  Agentic AI and Next Steps

title: 13  Agentic AI and Next Steps
text:
13.3 IDE integration
Some AI tools work inside the coding environment. GitHub Copilot is one common example. In RStudio, Copilot can suggest completions while you write code. It can be useful for repetitive syntax, common transformations, and quick examples.
IDE integration has two advantages:

It can use nearby code as context.
It reduces copying and pasting between the editor and a browser.

It also has risks:

Suggested code may look plausible but use the wrong variable.
It may silently introduce a package you did not plan to use.
It may choose a more complicated approach than needed.

Treat inline suggestions as drafts. Read them before accepting them, and run the code in small pieces.
crumbs: 13  Agentic AI and Next Steps

title: 13  Agentic AI and Next Steps
text:
13.4 RStudio-focused AI with gander
The gander package is an RStudio-oriented way to send selected code and surrounding context to an LLM. It can be configured to use different model providers through packages such as ellmer.
The exact setup depends on the model provider and your local environment, so the following examples are not executed.

options(.gander_chat = ellmer::chat_openai(model = "gpt-4.1-mini"))


options(.gander_chat = ellmer::chat_google_gemini(model = "gemini-2.5-flash"))

The important workflow is:

Select the code you want help with.
Ask a focused question.
Review the response before applying it.
Run the revised code.
Compare the output to what you expected.

Useful requests include:
Explain this pipeline one step at a time.
Suggest a clearer version of this plot, but keep the same data and variables.
Find likely bugs in this code. Do not change the statistical model.
crumbs: 13  Agentic AI and Next Steps

title: 13  Agentic AI and Next Steps
text:
13.5 Programmatic AI with ellmer
ellmer gives R users a more programmatic interface to language models. Instead of using a web page manually, you can make model calls from R code.
This can be useful for research workflows such as:

summarizing text fields
classifying open-ended survey responses
generating draft labels for qualitative coding
extracting structured information from documents
comparing the behavior of multiple prompts

Programmatic AI needs extra care. Save prompts, model names, settings, dates, and outputs when they are part of the research process. If an LLM contributes to an analysis, the workflow should be documented like any other computational step.

library(ellmer)

chat <- chat_openai(model = "gpt-4.1-mini")

chat$chat(
  "In one sentence, explain why direct labels can be easier to read than legends."
)

Do not send confidential, identifiable, or restricted data to a cloud model unless your data agreement and institutional rules allow it.
crumbs: 13  Agentic AI and Next Steps

title: 13  Agentic AI and Next Steps
text:
13.6 Local and cloud models
Cloud models run on servers provided by companies or institutions. Local models run on your own computer or a controlled server.
Cloud models often provide stronger performance and easier setup. They can be accessed through web interfaces, IDE tools, or APIs.
Local models can improve privacy and reproducibility because your data do not need to leave your machine and you can pin a specific model version. Tools such as Ollama make local models easier to run, but performance depends on hardware. Larger models may require a powerful GPU or a recent machine with enough memory.
The practical choice depends on the project:

Use cloud models for general learning, public examples, and low-sensitivity code help.
Consider local models for sensitive data, restricted research material, or workflows where you need control over the exact model.
Avoid sending private data, unpublished research data, student records, or confidential text to a public chatbot.
crumbs: 13  Agentic AI and Next Steps

title: 13  Agentic AI and Next Steps
text:
13.7 Agentic coding tools
Agentic tools go beyond chat. They can inspect files, run commands, edit code, run tests, and iterate. Examples include terminal or codebase agents such as Claude Code, Codex, Gemini CLI, and similar tools.
The difference from a chatbot is scope. A chatbot usually answers from pasted context. An agentic tool can work inside a project.
This makes agents useful for:

finding where a function or variable is defined
updating several related files
running checks after edits
comparing source and rendered output
writing tests or validation scripts
reviewing a project for missing files or missing packages

The same power creates risk. An agent can make broad changes quickly. Use boundaries.
Good instructions for an agent are explicit:
Edit only 07-lines-bars-and-annotation.qmd.
Do not modify data files or setup files.
Use files from the course project, especially files in Data/.
After editing, report exactly which files changed.
For code review:
Review this Quarto chapter for runnable R code, missing files, missing packages, and unclear explanations.
Do not edit files. List findings by severity with file and line references.
For debugging:
The render fails in 12-mapping.qmd.
Find the smallest change that fixes the render.
Do not change other chapters.
Explain the cause and the verification command.
crumbs: 13  Agentic AI and Next Steps

title: 13  Agentic AI and Next Steps
text:
13.8 A safe AI workflow
Use AI in small loops.

State the task and constraints.
Provide only the context needed.
Ask for a small change or diagnosis.
Inspect the answer before applying it.
Run the code.
Compare the output to the goal.
Commit or save only after verification.

This loop is slower than accepting the first suggestion, but it prevents many common errors.
crumbs: 13  Agentic AI and Next Steps

title: 13  Agentic AI and Next Steps
text:
13.9 How to ask better questions
A strong AI request includes the goal, context, constraints, and desired output.
Weak request:
Make this plot better.
Stronger request:
This plot compares life expectancy by continent in 2007 using Gapminder.
Suggest improvements that make the comparison clearer for a general audience.
Keep the data and chart type the same.
Return revised ggplot2 code and explain the changes briefly.
Weak request:
Fix my code.
Stronger request:
This Quarto chunk should read Data/gapminder/gapminder.csv and make a scatterplot.
It fails with the error below.
Identify the likely cause and propose the smallest code change.
Do not introduce new packages.

<paste error>
<paste code>
crumbs: 13  Agentic AI and Next Steps

title: 13  Agentic AI and Next Steps
text:
13.10 Reviewing AI output
Before using AI-generated code, check:

Does it use variables that exist?
Does it use packages that are loaded or installed?
Does it preserve the original research question?
Does it change the model, filter, or grouping without saying so?
Does it depend on files that are not included with the project?
Does it silently drop missing data?
Does the plot or table support the intended interpretation?
Can you explain every line?

If you cannot explain the code, ask the model to explain it or simplify it before using it.
crumbs: 13  Agentic AI and Next Steps

title: 13  Agentic AI and Next Steps
text:
13.11 Privacy, attribution, and reproducibility
AI use is part of the research workflow. Treat it like other research assistance.
Protect privacy:

Do not paste sensitive data into public tools.
Remove names, IDs, free-text responses, and confidential details unless sharing is allowed.
Check institutional, funder, journal, and IRB rules.

Support reproducibility:

Save important prompts when they affect analysis decisions.
Record model names when AI output is part of a method.
Keep AI-generated code in scripts or Quarto documents where it can be reviewed.
Prefer deterministic code over one-off manual AI interactions for repeated tasks.

Use attribution when appropriate. If AI contributed materially to text, code, or analysis, follow the disclosure rules for the course, journal, employer, or institution.
crumbs: 13  Agentic AI and Next Steps

title: 13  Agentic AI and Next Steps
text:
13.12 AI for visualization critique
AI can be useful as a first-pass critic of a figure. Ask it to evaluate the plot against specific criteria rather than asking whether it is “good.”
Review this visualization for a social science audience.
Focus on:
1. whether the chart type matches the comparison,
2. whether labels and legends are clear,
3. whether the color scale is appropriate,
4. whether the caption supports the main claim.

Do not invent facts beyond what is visible in the plot.
When possible, provide the code and the rendered figure. The code shows how the chart was made. The image shows what the reader sees.
crumbs: 13  Agentic AI and Next Steps

title: 13  Agentic AI and Next Steps
text:
13.13 AI for debugging Quarto projects
Quarto errors often come from one of a few causes:

a missing package
a data file that does not exist
a chunk that depends on an object created in a different chapter
a typo in a variable name
output written to a missing folder
internet-dependent code running during render
YAML indentation errors

A useful AI debugging prompt is specific:
This Quarto render fails.
Here is the error message and the chunk before it.
Please identify the most likely cause and propose one minimal fix.
If the problem is a missing data file, suggest how to check it in R.
Then verify the answer in R:

file.exists("Data/cigarettes/cigarettes.csv")


renv::status()


quarto::quarto_render("12-mapping.qmd")
crumbs: 13  Agentic AI and Next Steps

title: 13  Agentic AI and Next Steps
text:
13.14 Next steps after the course
The course has covered a full workflow:

import data
tidy and transform data
build plots with ggplot2
separate and compare groups
choose chart types
annotate and highlight
create tables
visualize model output
make maps
use interactivity and Quarto output formats
use AI assistance critically

The next step is to apply the workflow to one project you care about.
Choose a dataset and make a small portfolio:

One exploratory table.
One polished static plot.
One model visualization or map, if appropriate.
One short Quarto report explaining the question, data, code, and result.

Keep the project portable. Keep data inside the project, put code in scripts or Quarto files, and render the report from a clean session.

title: 13  Agentic AI and Next Steps
text:
13.15 Short exercise
Choose one plot or table from an earlier chapter.

Write a prompt asking an AI tool to critique it.
Include at least three constraints in the prompt.
Ask for suggestions, not a full rewrite.
Decide which suggestions you would accept, reject, or test.
Write two sentences explaining your decision.
crumbs: 13  Agentic AI and Next Steps

title: 13  Agentic AI and Next Steps
text:
13.16 Final checklist
Before sharing a visualization project, check:

The project renders from a clean session.
Data files are included with the project.
Required packages are listed.
Data sources are documented.
Tables and figures have clear titles, labels, and captions.
Optional internet/API steps are marked clearly.
AI-assisted code has been reviewed and tested.
The final report explains both what you found and how you produced it.

The purpose of the workflow is not just to make a figure. It is to make a figure that another person can understand, evaluate, and reproduce.
